Afghanistan is located in Southern-Central Asia.[[234]](#cite_note-245)[[235]](#cite_note-246)[[236]](#cite_note-247)[[237]](#cite_note-248)[[238]](#cite_note-249) The region centered in Afghanistan is considered the "crossroads of Asia",[[239]](#cite_note-250) and the country has had the nickname Heart of Asia.[[240]](#cite_note-251) The renowned [Urdu](/wiki/Urdu "Urdu") poet [Allama Iqbal](/wiki/Allama_Iqbal "Allama Iqbal") once wrote about the country:

> Asia is a body of water and earth, of which the Afghan nation is the heart. From its discord, the discord of Asia; and from its accord, the accord of Asia.

At over 652,864 km2 (252,072 sq mi),[[241]](#cite_note-252) Afghanistan is the world's [41st largest country](/wiki/List_of_countries_and_dependencies_by_area "List of countries and dependencies by area").[[242]](#cite_note-253) It is slightly bigger than France and smaller than Myanmar, and about the size of Texas in the United States. There is no coastline, as Afghanistan is [landlocked](/wiki/Landlocked "Landlocked"). Afghanistan shares its longest land border (the [Durand Line](/wiki/Durand_Line "Durand Line")) with Pakistan to the east and south, followed by borders with Tajikistan to the northeast, Iran to the west, Turkmenistan to the north-west, Uzbekistan to the north, and China to the far northeast; India claims a border with Afghanistan through Pakistani-administered [Kashmir](/wiki/Kashmir "Kashmir").[[243]](#cite_note-254) Clockwise from south-west, Afghanistan shares borders with the [Sistan and Baluchestan Province](/wiki/Sistan_and_Baluchestan_Province "Sistan and Baluchestan Province"), South Khorasan Province, and [Razavi Khorasan Province](/wiki/Razavi_Khorasan_Province "Razavi Khorasan Province") of Iran; [Ahal Region](/wiki/Ahal_Region "Ahal Region"), [Mary Region](/wiki/Mary_Region "Mary Region"), and [Lebap Region](/wiki/Lebap_Region "Lebap Region") of Turkmenistan; [Surxondaryo Region](/wiki/Surxondaryo_Region "Surxondaryo Region") of Uzbekistan; [Khatlon Region](/wiki/Khatlon_Region "Khatlon Region") and [Gorno-Badakhshan Autonomous Region](/wiki/Gorno-Badakhshan "Gorno-Badakhshan") of Tajikistan; [Xinjiang Uyghur Autonomous Region](/wiki/Xinjiang "Xinjiang") of China; and the [Gilgit-Baltistan territory](/wiki/Gilgit-Baltistan "Gilgit-Baltistan"), [Khyber Pakhtunkhwa province](/wiki/Khyber_Pakhtunkhwa "Khyber Pakhtunkhwa"), and [Balochistan province](/wiki/Balochistan,_Pakistan "Balochistan, Pakistan") of Pakistan.[[244]](#cite_note-255)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3b/FrontLines_Environment_Photo_Contest_Winner_-5_%285808476109%29.jpg/250px-FrontLines_Environment_Photo_Contest_Winner_-5_%285808476109%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:FrontLines_Environment_Photo_Contest_Winner_-5_(5808476109).jpg)

Floodplain cultivation in the [Wakhan Corridor](/wiki/Wakhan_Corridor "Wakhan Corridor"), [Pamir Mountains](/wiki/Pamir_Mountains "Pamir Mountains")

The geography in Afghanistan is varied, but is mostly mountainous and rugged, with some unusual mountain ridges accompanied by plateaus and river basins.[[245]](#cite_note-Fisher-2002-256) It is dominated by the [Hindu Kush](/wiki/Hindu_Kush "Hindu Kush") range, the western extension of the [Himalayas](/wiki/Himalayas "Himalayas") that stretches to eastern [Tibet](/wiki/Tibet "Tibet") via the [Pamir Mountains](/wiki/Pamir_Mountains "Pamir Mountains") and [Karakoram](/wiki/Karakoram "Karakoram") Mountains in Afghanistan's far north-east. Most of the highest points are in the east, consisting of fertile mountain valleys, often considered part of the "[Roof of the World](/wiki/Roof_of_the_World "Roof of the World")". The Hindu Kush ends at the west-central highlands, creating plains in the north and southwest, namely the [Turkestan](/wiki/Turkestan "Turkestan") Plains and the [Sistan Basin](/wiki/Sistan_Basin "Sistan Basin"); these two regions consist of rolling grasslands and semi-deserts, as well as hot, windy deserts, respectively.[[246]](#cite_note-257) Forests exist in the corridor between the provinces of [Nuristan](/wiki/Nuristan_Province "Nuristan Province") and [Paktika](/wiki/Paktika_Province "Paktika Province") (see [East Afghan montane conifer forests](/wiki/East_Afghan_montane_conifer_forests "East Afghan montane conifer forests")),[[247]](#cite_note-258) and [tundra](/wiki/Tundra "Tundra") in the northeast. The country's highest point is [Noshaq](/wiki/Noshaq "Noshaq"), at 7,492 m (24,580 ft) above sea level.[[248]](#cite_note-Factbook-259) The lowest point is located in [Jowzjan Province](/wiki/Jowzjan_Province "Jowzjan Province") along the Amu River bank, at 258 m (846 ft) above sea level.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/45/Afghan_topo_en.jpg/500px-Afghan_topo_en.jpg)](/wiki/File:Afghan_topo_en.jpg)

The mountainous [topography](/wiki/Topography "Topography") of Afghanistan

Despite having numerous rivers and [reservoirs](/wiki/List_of_dams_and_reservoirs_in_Afghanistan "List of dams and reservoirs in Afghanistan"), large parts of the country are dry. The [endorheic](/wiki/Endorheic "Endorheic") Sistan Basin is one of the world's driest regions.[[249]](#cite_note-260) The [Amu Darya](/wiki/Amu_Darya "Amu Darya") rises in the north of the Hindu Kush, while the nearby [Hari River](/wiki/Hari_(Afghanistan) "Hari (Afghanistan)") flows west towards [Herat](/wiki/Herat "Herat"), and the [Arghandab River](/wiki/Arghandab_River "Arghandab River") flows from the central region southwards. To the south and west of the Hindu Kush flow a number of streams that are tributaries of the [Indus River](/wiki/Indus_River "Indus River"),[[245]](#cite_note-Fisher-2002-256) such as the [Helmand River](/wiki/Helmand_River "Helmand River"). The [Kabul River](/wiki/Kabul_River "Kabul River") flows in an easterly direction to the Indus, ending at the Indian Ocean.[[250]](#cite_note-afghanistans.com-1-261) Afghanistan receives heavy snow during the winter in the Hindu Kush and Pamir Mountains, and the melting snow in the spring season enters the [rivers, lakes, and streams](/wiki/List_of_rivers_of_Afghanistan "List of rivers of Afghanistan").[[251]](#cite_note-262)[[252]](#cite_note-263) However, two-thirds of the country's water flows into the neighboring countries of Iran, Pakistan, and [Turkmenistan](/wiki/Turkmenistan "Turkmenistan"). As reported in 2010, the state needs more than US$2 billion to rehabilitate its irrigation systems so that the water is properly managed.[[253]](#cite_note-264)

In Afghanistan, [forest cover](/wiki/Forest_cover "Forest cover") accounts for approximately 2% of the total land area, equivalent to 1,208,440 hectares (ha) of forest in 2020, which remained unchanged since 1990. Of the naturally regenerating forest, 0% was reported to be [primary forest](/wiki/Primary_forest "Primary forest") (consisting of native tree species with no clearly visible indications of human activity), and around 0% of the forest area was found within protected areas. For the year 2015, 100% of the forest area was reported to be under [public ownership](/wiki/State_ownership "State ownership").[[254]](#cite_note-265)[[255]](#cite_note-266)

The northeastern Hindu Kush [mountain range](/wiki/Mountain_range "Mountain range"), located in and around the [Badakhshan Province](/wiki/Badakhshan_Province "Badakhshan Province") of Afghanistan, is situated in a [geologically active](/wiki/Natural_environment#Geological_activity "Natural environment") area where earthquakes occur almost every year.[[256]](#cite_note-267) They can be deadly and destructive, causing [landslides](/wiki/Landslide "Landslide") in some parts or [avalanches](/wiki/2009_Afghan_avalanches "2009 Afghan avalanches") during the winter.[[257]](#cite_note-268) In June 2022, a [destructive 5.9 earthquake](/wiki/June_2022_Afghanistan_earthquake "June 2022 Afghanistan earthquake") struck near the border with Pakistan, killing at least 1,150 people and sparking fears of a major humanitarian crisis.[[258]](#cite_note-269) On 7 October 2023, a [6.3 magnitude earthquake](/wiki/October_2023_Afghanistan_earthquake "October 2023 Afghanistan earthquake") struck northwest of Herat, killing over 1,400 people.[[259]](#cite_note-270)

### Climate

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e0/Koppen-Geiger_Map_v2_AFG_1991%E2%80%932020.svg/250px-Koppen-Geiger_Map_v2_AFG_1991%E2%80%932020.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Koppen-Geiger_Map_v2_AFG_1991%E2%80%932020.svg)

[Köppen climate map](/wiki/K%C3%B6ppen_climate_classification "Köppen climate classification") of Afghanistan[[260]](#cite_note-271)

Afghanistan has a [continental climate](/wiki/Continental_climate "Continental climate") with harsh winters in the [central highlands](/wiki/Hazarajat "Hazarajat"), the glaciated northeast (around [Nuristan](/wiki/Nuristan "Nuristan")), and the [Wakhan Corridor](/wiki/Wakhan_Corridor "Wakhan Corridor"), where the average temperature in January is below −15 °C (5 °F) and can reach −26 °C (−15 °F),[[245]](#cite_note-Fisher-2002-256) and hot summers in the low-lying areas of the [Sistan Basin](/wiki/Sistan_Basin "Sistan Basin") of the southwest, the [Jalalabad](/wiki/Jalalabad "Jalalabad") basin in the east, and the [Turkestan](/wiki/Afghan_Turkestan "Afghan Turkestan") plains along the [Amu River](/wiki/Amu_River "Amu River") in the north, where temperatures average over 35 °C (95 °F) in July[[248]](#cite_note-Factbook-259)[[261]](#cite_note-272) and can go over 43 °C (109 °F).[[245]](#cite_note-Fisher-2002-256) The country is generally [arid](/wiki/Arid "Arid") in the summers, with most rainfall falling between December and April. The lower areas of northern and western Afghanistan are the driest, with precipitation more common in the east. Although proximate to India, Afghanistan is mostly outside the [monsoon](/wiki/Monsoon "Monsoon") zone,[[245]](#cite_note-Fisher-2002-256) except the [Nuristan Province](/wiki/Nuristan_Province "Nuristan Province"), which occasionally receives summer monsoon rain.[[262]](#cite_note-273)

Although Afghanistan has contributed minimally to global [greenhouse gas emissions](/wiki/Greenhouse_gas_emissions "Greenhouse gas emissions"), it is one of the most vulnerable countries to [climate change](/wiki/Climate_change "Climate change") and least prepared to cope with its impacts.[[263]](#cite_note-274) [Climate change in Afghanistan](/wiki/Climate_change_in_Afghanistan "Climate change in Afghanistan") is causing more frequent and severe droughts.[[264]](#cite_note-275)[[265]](#cite_note-276) Severe drought conditions affect 25 of the country's 34 provinces, impacting over half the population.[[266]](#cite_note-:43-277) These droughts cause [desertification](/wiki/Desertification "Desertification"),[[266]](#cite_note-:43-277)[[267]](#cite_note-278) reduce [food](/wiki/Food_security "Food security")[[268]](#cite_note-279) and [water security](/wiki/Water_security "Water security"),[[269]](#cite_note-280) disrupt agriculture, and cause [internal displacement](/wiki/Internally_displaced_person "Internally displaced person").[[270]](#cite_note-actionaid.org-281) Extreme rainfall over short periods is also more likely, increasing the risk of [floods](/wiki/Flood "Flood") and [landslides](/wiki/Landslide "Landslide").[[271]](#cite_note-282) Due to rising temperatures, almost 14% of Afghanistan's glacier coverage was lost between 1990 and 2015,[[272]](#cite_note-283) increasing the risk of [glacial lake outburst floods](/wiki/Glacial_lake_outburst_flood "Glacial lake outburst flood").[[273]](#cite_note-284) By 2050, climate change could displace an additional 5 million people within Afghanistan.[[270]](#cite_note-actionaid.org-281)[[274]](#cite_note-:2-285)

### Biodiversity

Main article: [Wildlife of Afghanistan](/wiki/Wildlife_of_Afghanistan "Wildlife of Afghanistan")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a9/Schneeleopard-_P1020498.jpg/250px-Schneeleopard-_P1020498.jpg)](/wiki/File:Schneeleopard-_P1020498.jpg)

The [snow leopard](/wiki/Snow_leopard "Snow leopard") is the official national animal of Afghanistan.

Several types of mammals exist throughout Afghanistan. [Snow leopards](/wiki/Snow_leopard "Snow leopard"), [Siberian tigers](/wiki/Siberian_tiger "Siberian tiger") and [brown bears](/wiki/Brown_bear "Brown bear") live in the high elevation [alpine tundra](/wiki/Alpine_tundra "Alpine tundra") regions. The [Marco Polo sheep](/wiki/Marco_Polo_sheep "Marco Polo sheep") exclusively live in the Wakhan Corridor region of north-east Afghanistan. Foxes, [wolves](/wiki/Wolves "Wolves"), [otters](/wiki/Otter "Otter"), [deer](/wiki/Deer "Deer"), [wild sheep](/wiki/Wild_sheep "Wild sheep"), [lynx](/wiki/Lynx "Lynx") and other big cats populate the mountain forest region of the east. In the semi-desert northern plains, wildlife include a variety of birds, [hedgehogs](/wiki/Hedgehog "Hedgehog"), [gophers](/wiki/Gopher "Gopher"), and large carnivores such as [jackals](/wiki/Golden_jackal "Golden jackal") and [hyenas](/wiki/Striped_hyena "Striped hyena").[[275]](#cite_note-Gritzner-2009-286)

[Gazelles](/wiki/Gazelle "Gazelle"), [wild pigs](/wiki/Wild_boar "Wild boar") and jackals populate the [steppe](/wiki/Steppe "Steppe") plains of the south and west, while [mongoose](/wiki/Mongoose "Mongoose") and cheetahs exist in the semi-desert south.[[275]](#cite_note-Gritzner-2009-286) [Marmots](/wiki/Marmot "Marmot") and [ibex](/wiki/Ibex "Ibex") also live in the high mountains of Afghanistan, and [pheasants](/wiki/Pheasant "Pheasant") exist in some parts of the country.[[276]](#cite_note-287) The [Afghan hound](/wiki/Afghan_hound "Afghan hound") is a native breed of dog known for its fast speed and its long hair; it is relatively known in the west.[[277]](#cite_note-Wahab-2007-288)

[Endemic](/wiki/Endemic "Endemic") fauna of Afghanistan includes the [Afghan flying squirrel](/wiki/Afghan_flying_squirrel "Afghan flying squirrel"), [Afghan snowfinch](/wiki/Afghan_snowfinch "Afghan snowfinch"), *[Paradactylodon](/wiki/Paradactylodon "Paradactylodon")* (or the "[Paghman](/wiki/Paghman "Paghman") mountain salamander"), *[Stigmella kasyi](/wiki/Stigmella_kasyi "Stigmella kasyi")*, *[Vulcaniella kabulensis](/wiki/Vulcaniella_kabulensis "Vulcaniella kabulensis")*, [Afghan leopard gecko](/wiki/Afghan_leopard_gecko "Afghan leopard gecko"), *[Wheeleria parviflorellus](/wiki/Wheeleria_parviflorellus "Wheeleria parviflorellus")*, among others. Endemic flora include *[Iris afghanica](/wiki/Iris_afghanica "Iris afghanica")*. Afghanistan has a wide variety of birds despite its relatively arid climate – an estimated 460 species of which 235 breed within.[[277]](#cite_note-Wahab-2007-288)

The forest region of Afghanistan has vegetation such as [pine trees](/wiki/Pine_tree "Pine tree"), [spruce trees](/wiki/Spruce_tree "Spruce tree"), [fir trees](/wiki/Fir_tree "Fir tree") and [larches](/wiki/Larch "Larch"), whereas the steppe grassland regions consist of [broadleaf trees](/wiki/Broadleaf_tree "Broadleaf tree"), short grass, [perennial plants](/wiki/Perennial_plant "Perennial plant") and [shrublands](/wiki/Shrubland "Shrubland"). The colder high elevation regions are composed of hardy grasses and small flowering plants.[[275]](#cite_note-Gritzner-2009-286) Several regions are designated [protected areas](/wiki/List_of_protected_areas_of_Afghanistan "List of protected areas of Afghanistan"); there are three [national parks](/wiki/National_parks "National parks"): [Band-e Amir](/wiki/Band-e_Amir "Band-e Amir"), [Wakhan](/wiki/Wakhan_National_Park "Wakhan National Park") and [Nuristan](/wiki/Nuristan_National_Park "Nuristan National Park"). Afghanistan had a 2018 [Forest Landscape Integrity Index](/wiki/Forest_Landscape_Integrity_Index "Forest Landscape Integrity Index") mean score of 8.85/10, ranking it 15th globally out of 172 countries.[[278]](#cite_note-289)

## Government and politics

Main articles: [Government of Afghanistan](/wiki/Government_of_Afghanistan "Government of Afghanistan") and [Politics of Afghanistan](/wiki/Politics_of_Afghanistan "Politics of Afghanistan")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8c/200229-D-AP390-1529_%2849603221753%29.jpg/250px-200229-D-AP390-1529_%2849603221753%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:200229-D-AP390-1529_(49603221753).jpg)

[The Arg](/wiki/Arg,_Kabul "Arg, Kabul") (the Presidential palace) in Kabul, photographed in 2020

Following the effective collapse of the [Islamic Republic of Afghanistan](/wiki/Islamic_Republic_of_Afghanistan "Islamic Republic of Afghanistan") during the [2021 Taliban offensive](/wiki/2021_Taliban_offensive "2021 Taliban offensive"), the Taliban declared the country an Islamic Emirate. A new caretaker government was announced on 7 September.[[279]](#cite_note-BBCHardliners-290) As of July 2025[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Afghanistan&action=edit), only the [Russian Federation](/wiki/Russian_Federation "Russian Federation") has [recognized the Islamic Emirate of Afghanistan](/wiki/Recognition_of_the_Islamic_Emirate_of_Afghanistan "Recognition of the Islamic Emirate of Afghanistan") as the *[de jure](/wiki/De_jure "De jure")* government of Afghanistan.[[280]](#cite_note-Trevelyan-291) According to the [V-Dem Democracy indices](/wiki/V-Dem_Democracy_indices "V-Dem Democracy indices") Afghanistan in 2023 was the third least [electoral democratic country in Asia](/wiki/Democracy_in_Asia "Democracy in Asia").[[281]](#cite_note-292) Afghanistan operates on a centralised [unitary form of government](/wiki/Unitary_state "Unitary state").[[282]](#cite_note-Unitary-293)

A traditional instrument of governance in Afghanistan is the *[loya jirga](/wiki/Loya_jirga "Loya jirga")* (grand assembly), a Pashtun consultative meeting that was mainly organized for choosing a new [head of state](/wiki/Head_of_state "Head of state"), adopting a new constitution, or to settle national or regional issue such as war.[[283]](#cite_note-294) Loya jirgas have been held since at least 1747,[[284]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBarfield2012295-295) with [the most recent one](/wiki/Afghanistan_Ulema_gathering "Afghanistan Ulema gathering") occurring from June to July 2022.[[285]](#cite_note-296)[[286]](#cite_note-297)[[287]](#cite_note-298)

### Development of Taliban government

See also: [Akhund cabinet](/wiki/Akhund_cabinet "Akhund cabinet")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c4/Hasan_Akhund.png/250px-Hasan_Akhund.png)](/wiki/File:Hasan_Akhund.png)

First [Deputy Leader](/wiki/Deputy_Leader_of_the_Islamic_Emirate_of_Afghanistan "Deputy Leader of the Islamic Emirate of Afghanistan") and [Interior Minister](/wiki/Interior_Minister_of_Afghanistan "Interior Minister of Afghanistan")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/4/41/Mullah_Yaqoob_in_January_2022.png)](/wiki/File:Mullah_Yaqoob_in_January_2022.png)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/af/Abdul_Ghani_Baradar.jpg/250px-Abdul_Ghani_Baradar.jpg)](/wiki/File:Abdul_Ghani_Baradar.jpg)

[Abdul Ghani Baradar](/wiki/Abdul_Ghani_Baradar "Abdul Ghani Baradar")  
Third Deputy Leader and First [Deputy Prime Minister](/wiki/Deputy_Prime_Minister_of_Afghanistan "Deputy Prime Minister of Afghanistan")

On 17 August 2021, the leader of the Taliban-affiliated [Hezb-e-Islami Gulbuddin](/wiki/Hezb-e_Islami_Gulbuddin "Hezb-e Islami Gulbuddin") party, [Gulbuddin Hekmatyar](/wiki/Gulbuddin_Hekmatyar "Gulbuddin Hekmatyar"), met with both [Hamid Karzai](/wiki/Hamid_Karzai "Hamid Karzai"), the former [President of Afghanistan](/wiki/President_of_Afghanistan "President of Afghanistan"), and [Abdullah Abdullah](/wiki/Abdullah_Abdullah "Abdullah Abdullah"), the former chairman of the High Council for National Reconciliation and former [Chief Executive](/wiki/Chief_Executive_(Afghanistan) "Chief Executive (Afghanistan)"), in [Doha](/wiki/Doha "Doha"), Qatar, with the aim of forming a [national unity government](/wiki/National_unity_government "National unity government").[[288]](#cite_note-299)[[289]](#cite_note-300) President Ashraf Ghani, having fled the country during the Taliban advance to either Tajikistan or [Uzbekistan](/wiki/Uzbekistan "Uzbekistan"), emerged in the United Arab Emirates and said that he supported such negotiations and was in talks to return to Afghanistan.[[290]](#cite_note-301)[[291]](#cite_note-302) Many figures within the Taliban generally agreed that continuation of the [2004 Constitution of Afghanistan](/wiki/2004_Constitution_of_Afghanistan "2004 Constitution of Afghanistan") may, if correctly applied, be workable as the basis for the new religious state as their objections to the former government were political, and not religious.[[292]](#cite_note-303)

Hours after the final flight of American troops left Kabul on 30 August, a Taliban official interviewed said that a new government would likely be announced as early as Friday 3 September after [Jumu'ah](/wiki/Jumu%27ah "Jumu'ah"). It was added that Hibatullah Akhundzada would be officially named [Emir](/wiki/Head_of_the_Islamic_Emirate_of_Afghanistan "Head of the Islamic Emirate of Afghanistan"), with cabinet ministers being revealed at the [Arg](/wiki/Arg_(Kabul) "Arg (Kabul)") in an official ceremony. Abdul Ghani Baradar would be named [head of government](/wiki/Head_of_government "Head of government") as [Prime Minister](/wiki/Prime_Minister_of_Afghanistan "Prime Minister of Afghanistan"), while other important positions would go to [Sirajuddin Haqqani](/wiki/Sirajuddin_Haqqani "Sirajuddin Haqqani") and [Mullah Yaqoob](/wiki/Mullah_Yaqoob "Mullah Yaqoob"). Beneath the supreme leader, day-to-day governance will be entrusted to the [cabinet](/wiki/Cabinet_of_Afghanistan#Islamic_Emirate_(2021–present) "Cabinet of Afghanistan").[[293]](#cite_note-304)

In a report by CNN-News18, sources said the new government was going to be governed similarly to Iran with Hibatullah Akhundzada as supreme leader similar to the role of [Saayid Ali Khamenei](/wiki/Ali_Khamenei "Ali Khamenei"), and would be based out of [Kandahar](/wiki/Kandahar "Kandahar"). Baradar or Yaqoob would be head of government as Prime minister. The government's ministries and agencies will be under a cabinet presided over by the Prime Minister. The Supreme Leader would preside over an [executive body](/wiki/Executive_(government) "Executive (government)") known as the Supreme Council with anywhere from 11 to 72 members. [Abdul Hakim Haqqani](/wiki/Abdul_Hakim_Haqqani "Abdul Hakim Haqqani") is likely to be promoted to [Chief justice](/wiki/Chief_justice "Chief justice"). According to the report, the new government will take place within the framework of an amended [1964 Constitution of Afghanistan](/wiki/1964_Constitution_of_Afghanistan "1964 Constitution of Afghanistan").[[294]](#cite_note-305) Government formation was delayed due to concerns about forming a broad-based government acceptable to the international community.[[295]](#cite_note-306) It was later added however that the Taliban's Rahbari Shura, the group's leadership council was divided between the hardline Haqqani Network and moderate Abdul Ghani Baradar over appointments needed to form an "inclusive" government. Reports claimed that this culminated in a skirmish which led to Baradar being injured and treated in Pakistan, however this was denied by Baradar himself.[[296]](#cite_note-307)[[297]](#cite_note-308)

As of early September 2021 the Taliban were planning the cabinet to be men-only. Journalists and other human rights activists, mostly women, [protested](/wiki/2021_Afghan_protests "2021 Afghan protests") in Herat and Kabul, calling for women to be included.[[298]](#cite_note-309) The [acting Cabinet](/wiki/Cabinet_of_Afghanistan#Islamic_Emirate_(2021–present) "Cabinet of Afghanistan") announced on 7 September was men-only, and the [Ministry of Women's Affairs](/wiki/Ministry_of_Women%27s_Affairs_(Afghanistan) "Ministry of Women's Affairs (Afghanistan)") was abolished.[[279]](#cite_note-BBCHardliners-290)

As of July 2025, only [Russia](/wiki/Russia "Russia") has recognized the Taliban government as the legitimate authorities of Afghanistan.[[280]](#cite_note-Trevelyan-291) The U.N has stated that recognition was impossible so long as restrictions on female education and employment remained.[[299]](#cite_note-:1-310)[[300]](#cite_note-311) On 16 September 2024, the Taliban suspended polio vaccination campaigns in Afghanistan, as reported by the United Nations, posing a significant risk to global polio eradication efforts.[[301]](#cite_note-312) In a decree by Hibatullah Akhundzada on 15 August, the description of the government as interim was dropped, with all ministers remaining in their posts, but on a permanent rather than acting basis.[[302]](#cite_note-313)[[303]](#cite_note-314)

### Administrative divisions

Main articles: [Provinces of Afghanistan](/wiki/Provinces_of_Afghanistan "Provinces of Afghanistan") and [Districts of Afghanistan](/wiki/Districts_of_Afghanistan "Districts of Afghanistan")

Afghanistan is administratively divided into 34 provinces (*[wilayat](/wiki/Wilayat "Wilayat")*).[[304]](#cite_note-315) Each province has a governor and a capital. The country is further divided into nearly 400 provincial [districts](/wiki/Districts_of_Afghanistan "Districts of Afghanistan"), each of which normally covers a city or several villages. Each district is represented by a district governor.

The [provincial governors](/wiki/List_of_current_governors_of_Afghanistan "List of current governors of Afghanistan") are now appointed by the [Prime Minister of Afghanistan](/wiki/Prime_Minister_of_Afghanistan "Prime Minister of Afghanistan"), and the district governors are selected by the provincial governors.[[305]](#cite_note-316) The provincial governors are representatives of the central government in Kabul and are responsible for all administrative and formal issues within their provinces. There are also provincial councils that are elected through direct and general elections for four years.[[306]](#cite_note-317) The functions of provincial councils are to take part in provincial development planning and to participate in the monitoring and appraisal of other provincial governance institutions.

According to article 140 of the constitution and the presidential decree on electoral law, mayors of cities should be elected through free and direct elections for a four-year term. In practice however, mayors are appointed by the government.[[307]](#cite_note-318)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/44/Afghanistan_provinces_numbered.png/500px-Afghanistan_provinces_numbered.png)](/wiki/File:Afghanistan_provinces_numbered.png)

### Foreign relations

Main article: [Foreign relations of Afghanistan](/wiki/Foreign_relations_of_Afghanistan "Foreign relations of Afghanistan")

See also: [Ministry of Foreign Affairs (Afghanistan)](/wiki/Ministry_of_Foreign_Affairs_(Afghanistan) "Ministry of Foreign Affairs (Afghanistan)")

Afghanistan became a member of the United Nations in 1946.[[308]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDupree1997642-319) Historically, Afghanistan had strong relations with Germany, one of the first countries to recognize Afghanistan's independence in 1919; the Soviet Union, which provided much aid and military training for Afghanistan's forces and includes the signing of a Treaty of Friendship in 1921 and 1978; and India, with which a friendship treaty was signed in 1950.[[309]](#cite_note-320) Relations with Pakistan have often been tense for various reasons such as the Durand Line border issue and alleged Pakistani involvement in Afghan insurgent groups.

The present Islamic Emirate of Afghanistan is currently [partially recognized](/wiki/Recognition_of_the_Islamic_Emirate_of_Afghanistan "Recognition of the Islamic Emirate of Afghanistan"), but has had notable unofficial ties with China, Pakistan, and Qatar.[[310]](#cite_note-321)[[311]](#cite_note-322) Under the previous Islamic Republic of Afghanistan, it enjoyed cordial relations with a number of [NATO](/wiki/NATO "NATO") and allied nations, particularly the [United States](/wiki/Afghanistan%E2%80%93United_States_relations "Afghanistan–United States relations"), [Canada](/wiki/Afghanistan%E2%80%93Canada_relations "Afghanistan–Canada relations"), [United Kingdom](/wiki/Afghanistan%E2%80%93United_Kingdom_relations "Afghanistan–United Kingdom relations"), [Germany](/wiki/Afghanistan%E2%80%93Germany_relations "Afghanistan–Germany relations"), Australia, and [Turkey](/wiki/Afghanistan%E2%80%93Turkey_relations "Afghanistan–Turkey relations"). In 2012, the United States and the then-republic in Afghanistan signed their [Strategic Partnership Agreement](/wiki/US%E2%80%93Afghanistan_Strategic_Partnership_Agreement "US–Afghanistan Strategic Partnership Agreement") in which Afghanistan became a [major non-NATO ally](/wiki/Major_non-NATO_ally "Major non-NATO ally").[[312]](#cite_note-323) Such qualification was rescinded by US President [Joe Biden](/wiki/Joe_Biden "Joe Biden") in July 2022.[[313]](#cite_note-324)

### Military

Main article: [Afghan Armed Forces](/wiki/Afghan_Armed_Forces "Afghan Armed Forces")

The [Armed Forces of the Islamic Emirate of Afghanistan](/wiki/Armed_Forces_of_the_Islamic_Emirate_of_Afghanistan "Armed Forces of the Islamic Emirate of Afghanistan") captured a large amount of weapons, hardware, vehicles, aerocrafts, and equipment from the Afghan National Security Forces following the [2021 Taliban offensive](/wiki/2021_Taliban_offensive "2021 Taliban offensive") and the [Fall of Kabul](/wiki/Fall_of_Kabul_(2021) "Fall of Kabul (2021)"). The total value of the captured equipment has been estimated at US$83 billion.[[314]](#cite_note-325)[[315]](#cite_note-326)

### Human rights

Main article: [Human rights in Afghanistan](/wiki/Human_rights_in_Afghanistan "Human rights in Afghanistan")

Further information: [Women in Afghanistan](/wiki/Women_in_Afghanistan "Women in Afghanistan"), [LGBT rights in Afghanistan](/wiki/LGBT_rights_in_Afghanistan "LGBT rights in Afghanistan"), [Treatment of women by the Taliban](/wiki/Treatment_of_women_by_the_Taliban "Treatment of women by the Taliban"), and [Afghan refugees](/wiki/Afghan_refugees "Afghan refugees")

Homosexuality is [taboo](/wiki/Taboo "Taboo") in Afghan society;[[316]](#cite_note-327) according to the Penal Code, homosexual intimacy is punished by up to a year in prison.[[317]](#cite_note-328) Under [Sharia law](/wiki/Sharia_law "Sharia law") offenders can be [punished by death](/wiki/Death_penalty_for_homosexuality "Death penalty for homosexuality").[[318]](#cite_note-329)[[319]](#cite_note-330) However, an ancient tradition involving male homosexual acts between children and older men (typically wealthy warlords or elite people) called *[bacha bazi](/wiki/Bacha_bazi "Bacha bazi")* persists.

Religious minorities such as Sikhs,[[320]](#cite_note-331) Hindus,[[321]](#cite_note-332) and Christians have reportedly faced persecution.[[322]](#cite_note-333)[[323]](#cite_note-334)

Since May 2022, all women in Afghanistan have been required by law to wear full-body coverings when in public (either a [burqa](/wiki/Burqa "Burqa") or an [abaya](/wiki/Abaya "Abaya") paired with a [niqāb](/wiki/Niq%C4%81b "Niqāb"), which leaves only the eyes uncovered).[[324]](#cite_note-335)[[325]](#cite_note-336) First [Deputy Leader](/wiki/Deputy_Leader_of_the_Islamic_Emirate_of_Afghanistan "Deputy Leader of the Islamic Emirate of Afghanistan") [Sirajuddin Haqqani](/wiki/Sirajuddin_Haqqani "Sirajuddin Haqqani") claimed the decree is only advisory and no form of [hijab](/wiki/Hijab "Hijab") is compulsory in Afghanistan,[[326]](#cite_note-337) though this contradicts the reality.[[327]](#cite_note-338) It has been speculated that there is a genuine internal policy division over women's rights between hardliners, including leader Hibatullah Akhundzada, and pragmatists, though they publicly present a united front.[[328]](#cite_note-339) Another decree was issued shortly after the first, requiring female TV presenters to cover their faces during broadcasts.[[329]](#cite_note-340) Since the Taliban takeover, suicides among women have become more common, and the country could now be one of the few where the rate of suicide among women surpasses that among men.[[330]](#cite_note-341)[[331]](#cite_note-342)[[332]](#cite_note-343)

In May 2022, the Taliban dissolved Afghanistan's Human Rights Commission along with four other government departments, citing the country's budget deficit.[[333]](#cite_note-344)

In January 2025, the [International Criminal Court](/wiki/International_Criminal_Court "International Criminal Court") issued two warrants against the [Taliban supreme leader](/wiki/Supreme_Leader_of_Afghanistan "Supreme Leader of Afghanistan") Haibatullah Akhundzada and the [Chief judge](/wiki/Chief_Justice_of_Afghanistan "Chief Justice of Afghanistan"), Abdul Hakim Haqqani, for committing [crimes against humanity](/wiki/Crimes_against_humanity "Crimes against humanity") with the oppression and persecution of Afghan women and girls, and deprived of their freedom of movement, the rights to control their bodies, to education, and to a private and family life, while the alleged resistance and opposition were brutally suppressed with murder, imprisonment, torture, rape, and other forms of sexual violence, since 2021. ICC member states are obliged to arrest wanted persons if they are on their territory.[[334]](#cite_note-Taliban_warrent-345)

## Economy

Main article: [Economy of Afghanistan](/wiki/Economy_of_Afghanistan "Economy of Afghanistan")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/80/Afghan_pomegranate_processing.jpg/250px-Afghan_pomegranate_processing.jpg)](/wiki/File:Afghan_pomegranate_processing.jpg)

Workers processing [pomegranates](/wiki/Pomegranate_production_in_Afghanistan "Pomegranate production in Afghanistan") (*anaar*), for which Afghanistan is famous in Asia

Afghanistan's nominal GDP was $20.1 billion in 2020, or $81 billion by [purchasing power parity](/wiki/Purchasing_power_parity "Purchasing power parity") (PPP).[[335]](#cite_note-imf2-346) Its [GDP per capita](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_GDP_(PPP)_per_capita "List of countries by GDP (PPP) per capita") is $2,459 (PPP) and $611 by nominal.[[335]](#cite_note-imf2-346) Despite having $1 trillion or more in mineral deposits,[[336]](#cite_note-347) it remains one of the world's [least developed countries](/wiki/Least_developed_countries "Least developed countries"). Afghanistan's rough physical geography and its landlocked status has been cited as reasons why the country has always been among the least developed in the modern era – a factor where progress is also slowed by contemporary conflict and political instability.[[245]](#cite_note-Fisher-2002-256) The country imports over $7 billion worth of goods but exports only $784 million, mainly fruits and [nuts](/wiki/Nut_(fruit) "Nut (fruit)"). It has $2.8 billion in [external debt](/wiki/External_debt "External debt").[[248]](#cite_note-Factbook-259) The service sector contributed the most to the GDP (55.9%) followed by agriculture (23%) and industry (21.1%).[[337]](#cite_note-348)

[Da Afghanistan Bank](/wiki/Da_Afghanistan_Bank "Da Afghanistan Bank") serves as the central bank of the nation[[338]](#cite_note-349) and the [Afghani](/wiki/Afghan_afghani "Afghan afghani") (AFN) is the national currency, with an exchange rate of about 75 Afghanis to 1 US dollar.[[339]](#cite_note-350) A number of local and foreign banks operate in the country, including the [Afghanistan International Bank](/wiki/Afghanistan_International_Bank "Afghanistan International Bank"), [New Kabul Bank](/wiki/New_Kabul_Bank "New Kabul Bank"), [Azizi Bank](/wiki/Azizi_Bank "Azizi Bank"), [Pashtany Bank](/wiki/Pashtany_Bank "Pashtany Bank"), [Standard Chartered Bank](/wiki/Standard_Chartered_Bank "Standard Chartered Bank"), and the [First Micro Finance Bank](/wiki/First_MicroFinance_Bank-Afghanistan "First MicroFinance Bank-Afghanistan").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ec/Afghan_carpets_being_sold.jpg/500px-Afghan_carpets_being_sold.jpg)](/wiki/File:Afghan_carpets_being_sold.jpg)

[Afghan rugs](/wiki/Afghan_rugs "Afghan rugs") are one of Afghanistan's main exports.

In 2010, one of the main drivers for the economic recovery was the return of over 5 million [expatriates](/wiki/Afghan_diaspora "Afghan diaspora"), who brought with them entrepreneurship and wealth-creating skills as well as much needed funds to start up businesses. Many Afghans got involved in construction, which was one of the largest industries in the country at the time.[[340]](#cite_note-351) Some of the major national construction projects include the $35 billion New Kabul City next to the capital, the Aino Mena project in Kandahar, and the [Ghazi Amanullah Khan Town](/wiki/Ghazi_Amanullah_Khan_Town "Ghazi Amanullah Khan Town") near Jalalabad.[[341]](#cite_note-352)[[342]](#cite_note-353)[[343]](#cite_note-354) Similar development projects have also begun in [Herat](/wiki/Herat "Herat"), [Mazar-e-Sharif](/wiki/Mazar-e-Sharif "Mazar-e-Sharif"), and other cities.[[344]](#cite_note-355) An estimated 400,000 people enter the labor market each year.[[345]](#cite_note-356)

Several small companies and factories began operating in different parts of the country, which not only provide revenues to the government but also create new jobs. Improvements to the business environment have resulted in more than $1.5 billion in [telecom](/wiki/Telecommunications "Telecommunications") investment and created more than 100,000 jobs since 2003.[[346]](#cite_note-357) [Afghan rugs](/wiki/Afghan_rug "Afghan rug") are becoming popular again, allowing many carpet dealers around the country to hire more workers; in 2016–17 it was the fourth most exported group of items.[[347]](#cite_note-358)

Afghanistan is a member of [WTO](/wiki/WTO "WTO"), [SAARC](/wiki/SAARC "SAARC"), [ECO](/wiki/Economic_Cooperation_Organization "Economic Cooperation Organization"), and [OIC](/wiki/OIC "OIC"). It holds an observer status in [SCO](/wiki/Shanghai_Cooperation_Organisation "Shanghai Cooperation Organisation"). In 2018, a majority of imports come from either Iran, China, Pakistan and Kazakhstan, while 84% of exports are to Pakistan and India.[[348]](#cite_note-359)

Since the Taliban's takeover of the country in August 2021, the United States has [frozen about $9 billion](/wiki/Afghan_frozen_assets "Afghan frozen assets") in assets belonging to the [Afghan central bank](/wiki/Da_Afghanistan_Bank "Da Afghanistan Bank"),[[349]](#cite_note-360) blocking the Taliban from accessing billions of dollars held in US bank accounts.[[350]](#cite_note-361)[[351]](#cite_note-362)

The GDP of Afghanistan is estimated to have dropped by 20% following the Taliban return to power. Following this, after months of free-fall, the Afghan economy began stabilizing, as a result of the Taliban's restrictions on smuggled imports, limits on banking transactions, and [UN aid](/wiki/Foreign_aid_to_Afghanistan "Foreign aid to Afghanistan"). In 2023, the Afghan economy began seeing signs of revival. This has also been followed by stable exchange rates, low inflation, stable revenue collection, and the rise of trade in exports.[[352]](#cite_note-363) In the third quarter of 2023, the [Afghani](/wiki/Afghan_afghani "Afghan afghani") rose to be the best performing currency in the world, climbing over 9% against the [US dollar](/wiki/United_States_dollar "United States dollar").[[353]](#cite_note-364)

### Agriculture

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4b/Afghan_Saffron.jpg/250px-Afghan_Saffron.jpg)](/wiki/File:Afghan_Saffron.jpg)

Afghan [saffron](/wiki/Saffron "Saffron")

Agricultural production is the backbone of Afghanistan's economy[[354]](#cite_note-365) and has traditionally dominated the economy, employing about 40% of the workforce as of 2018.[[355]](#cite_note-366) The country is known for producing [pomegranates](/wiki/Pomegranate_production_in_Afghanistan "Pomegranate production in Afghanistan"), grapes, apricots, melons, and several other fresh and dry fruits. Afghanistan also became the world's top producer of [cannabis](/wiki/Cannabis "Cannabis") in 2010.[[356]](#cite_note-367) In March 2023, however, cannabis production was banned by a decree from Hibatullah Akhundzada.[[357]](#cite_note-368)

[Saffron](/wiki/Saffron "Saffron"), the most expensive spice, grows in Afghanistan, particularly [Herat Province](/wiki/Herat_Province "Herat Province"). In recent years, there has been an uptick in saffron production, which authorities and farmers are using to try to replace poppy cultivation. Between 2012 and 2019, the saffron cultivated and produced in Afghanistan was consecutively ranked the world's best by the International Taste and Quality Institute.[[358]](#cite_note-369)[[359]](#cite_note-370) Production hit record high in 2019 (19,469 kg of saffron), and one kilogram is sold domestically between $634 and $1147.[[360]](#cite_note-371)

The availability of cheap diesel-powered water pumps imported from China and Pakistan, and in the 2010s, of cheap solar power to pump water, resulted in expansion of agriculture and population in the southwestern deserts of Afghanistan in [Kandahar](/wiki/Kandahar_Province "Kandahar Province"), [Helmand](/wiki/Helmand_Province "Helmand Province") and [Nimruz](/wiki/Nimruz_Province "Nimruz Province") provinces in the 2010s. Wells have gradually been deepened, but water resources are limited. Opium is the major crop, but as of 2022, was under attack by the new Taliban government which, to suppress opium production, was systematically suppressing water pumping.[[361]](#cite_note-372)[[362]](#cite_note-373) In a 2023 report, poppy cultivation in southern Afghanistan was reduced by over 80% as a result of Taliban campaigns to stop its use toward opium. This included a 99% reduction of opium growth in the Helmand Province.[[363]](#cite_note-374) In November 2023, a U.N report showed that in the entirety of Afghanistan, poppy cultivation dropped by over 95%, removing it from its place as being the world's largest opium producer.[[364]](#cite_note-375)[[365]](#cite_note-376)

### Mining

Main article: [Mining in Afghanistan](/wiki/Mining_in_Afghanistan "Mining in Afghanistan")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/47/Lapis_Lazuli_from_Afghanistan.jpg/250px-Lapis_Lazuli_from_Afghanistan.jpg)](/wiki/File:Lapis_Lazuli_from_Afghanistan.jpg)

[Lapis lazuli](/wiki/Lapis_lazuli "Lapis lazuli") stones

The country's natural resources include: coal, copper, iron ore, [lithium](/wiki/Lithium "Lithium"), [uranium](/wiki/Uranium "Uranium"), [rare earth elements](/wiki/Rare_earth_element "Rare earth element"), [chromite](/wiki/Chromite "Chromite"), gold, [zinc](/wiki/Zinc "Zinc"), [talc](/wiki/Talc "Talc"), [barite](/wiki/Barite "Barite"), [sulfur](/wiki/Sulfur "Sulfur"), lead, [marble](/wiki/Marble "Marble"), precious and [semi-precious stones](/wiki/Semi-precious_stones "Semi-precious stones"), natural gas, and petroleum.[[366]](#cite_note-peters2007-377)[[367]](#cite_note-bgs-378) In 2010, US and Afghan government officials estimated that untapped mineral deposits located in 2007 by the [US Geological Survey](/wiki/US_Geological_Survey "US Geological Survey") are worth at least $1 trillion.[[368]](#cite_note-bbcminerals-379)

[Michael E. O'Hanlon](/wiki/Michael_E._O%27Hanlon "Michael E. O'Hanlon") of the [Brookings Institution](/wiki/Brookings_Institution "Brookings Institution") estimated that if Afghanistan generates about $10 billion per year from its [mineral deposits](/wiki/Mining_in_Afghanistan "Mining in Afghanistan"), its [gross national product](/wiki/Gross_national_product "Gross national product") would double and provide long-term funding for critical needs.[[369]](#cite_note-380) The [United States Geological Survey](/wiki/United_States_Geological_Survey "United States Geological Survey") (USGS) estimated in 2006 that northern Afghanistan has an average 460 million m3 (2.9 billion bbl) of [crude oil](/wiki/Crude_oil "Crude oil"), 440 billion m3 (15.7 trillion cu ft) of natural gas, and 67 billion L (562 million US bbl) of [natural gas liquids](/wiki/Natural_gas_liquids "Natural gas liquids").[[370]](#cite_note-381) In 2011, Afghanistan signed an oil exploration contract with [China National Petroleum Corporation](/wiki/China_National_Petroleum_Corporation "China National Petroleum Corporation") (CNPC) for the development of three oil fields along the Amu Darya river in the north.[[371]](#cite_note-382)

The country has significant amounts of [lithium](/wiki/Lithium "Lithium"), copper, gold, coal, iron ore, and other [minerals](/wiki/Minerals "Minerals").[[366]](#cite_note-peters2007-377)[[367]](#cite_note-bgs-378)[[372]](#cite_note-383) The [Khanashin](/wiki/Khanashin "Khanashin") [carbonatite](/wiki/Carbonatite "Carbonatite") in Helmand Province contains 1,000,000 tonnes (980,000 [long tons](/wiki/Long_ton "Long ton"); 1,100,000 [short tons](/wiki/Short_ton "Short ton")) of [rare earth elements](/wiki/Rare_earth_element "Rare earth element").[[373]](#cite_note-384) In 2007, a 30-year lease was granted for the [Aynak](/wiki/Mes_Aynak#Copper_Mine "Mes Aynak") copper mine to the [China Metallurgical Group](/wiki/China_Metallurgical_Group "China Metallurgical Group") for $3 billion,[[374]](#cite_note-385) making it the biggest foreign investment and private business venture in Afghanistan's history.[[375]](#cite_note-386) The state-run [Steel Authority of India](/wiki/Steel_Authority_of_India "Steel Authority of India") won the mining rights to develop the huge [Hajigak](/wiki/Hajigak_Pass "Hajigak Pass") iron ore deposit in central Afghanistan.[[376]](#cite_note-387) Government officials estimate that 30% of the country's untapped mineral deposits are worth at least $1 trillion.[[368]](#cite_note-bbcminerals-379) One official asserted that "this will become the backbone of the Afghan economy" and a Pentagon memo stated that Afghanistan could become the "Saudi Arabia of lithium".[[377]](#cite_note-388) The lithium reserves of 21 Mio. tons could amount to the ones of [Bolivia](/wiki/Bolivia "Bolivia"), which is currently viewed as the country with the largest lithium reserves.[[378]](#cite_note-Hosp-2021-389) Other larger deposits are the ones of [bauxite](/wiki/Bauxite "Bauxite") and [cobalt](/wiki/Cobalt "Cobalt").[[378]](#cite_note-Hosp-2021-389)

Access to [biocapacity](/wiki/Biocapacity "Biocapacity") in Afghanistan is lower than world average. In 2016, Afghanistan had 0.43 global hectares[[379]](#cite_note-footprintdata-390) of biocapacity per person within its territory, much less than the world average of 1.6 global hectares per person.[[380]](#cite_note-391) In 2016 Afghanistan used 0.73 global hectares of biocapacity per person—their [ecological footprint](/wiki/Ecological_footprint "Ecological footprint") of consumption. This means they use just under double as much biocapacity as Afghanistan contains. As a result, Afghanistan is running a biocapacity deficit.[[379]](#cite_note-footprintdata-390)

In September 2023, the [Taliban](/wiki/Taliban "Taliban") signed mining contracts worth $6.5 billion, with extractions based on gold, iron, lead, and zinc in the provinces of Herat, Ghor, Logar, and Takhar.[[381]](#cite_note-392)

### Energy

Main article: [Energy in Afghanistan](/wiki/Energy_in_Afghanistan "Energy in Afghanistan")

See also: [Renewable energy in Afghanistan](/wiki/Renewable_energy_in_Afghanistan "Renewable energy in Afghanistan")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0e/Afghanistan_electricity_production.svg/250px-Afghanistan_electricity_production.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Afghanistan_electricity_production.svg)

Afghanistan electricity supply (1980–2019)

According to the [World Bank](/wiki/World_Bank "World Bank"), 98% of the rural population have access to electricity in 2018, up from 28% in 2008.[[382]](#cite_note-393) Overall the figure stands at 98.7%.[[383]](#cite_note-394) As of 2016, Afghanistan produces 1,400 [megawatts](/wiki/Megawatt "Megawatt") of power, but still imports the majority of electricity via transmission lines from Iran and the Central Asian states.[[384]](#cite_note-395) The majority of electricity production is via [hydropower](/wiki/Hydropower "Hydropower"), helped by the amount of rivers and streams that flow from the mountains.[[385]](#cite_note-396) However electricity is not always reliable and blackouts happen, including in Kabul.[[386]](#cite_note-Afghanistan_Analysts_Network_–_English-2015-397) In recent years an increasing number of [solar](/wiki/Solar_power "Solar power"), [biomass](/wiki/Biomass "Biomass") and wind power plants have been constructed.[[387]](#cite_note-398) Currently under development are the [CASA-1000](/wiki/CASA-1000 "CASA-1000") project which will transmit electricity from Kyrgyzstan and Tajikistan, and the [Turkmenistan-Afghanistan-Pakistan-India](/wiki/Turkmenistan-Afghanistan-Pakistan-India_pipeline "Turkmenistan-Afghanistan-Pakistan-India pipeline") (TAPI) gas pipeline.[[386]](#cite_note-Afghanistan_Analysts_Network_–_English-2015-397) Power is managed by the [Da Afghanistan Breshna Sherkat](/w/index.php?title=Da_Afghanistan_Breshna_Sherkat&action=edit&redlink=1 "Da Afghanistan Breshna Sherkat (page does not exist)") (DABS, Afghanistan Electricity Company).

Important dams include the [Kajaki Dam](/wiki/Kajaki_Dam "Kajaki Dam"), [Dahla Dam](/wiki/Dahla_Dam "Dahla Dam"), and the [Sardeh Band Dam](/wiki/Sardeh_Band_Dam "Sardeh Band Dam").[[250]](#cite_note-afghanistans.com-1-261)

### Tourism

Main article: [Tourism in Afghanistan](/wiki/Tourism_in_Afghanistan "Tourism in Afghanistan")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f8/Lake_in_Band-e_Amir_National_Park%2C_March_2008.jpg/250px-Lake_in_Band-e_Amir_National_Park%2C_March_2008.jpg)](/wiki/File:Lake_in_Band-e_Amir_National_Park,_March_2008.jpg)

[Band-e Amir National Park](/wiki/Band-e_Amir_National_Park "Band-e Amir National Park")

Tourism is a small industry in Afghanistan due to security issues. Nevertheless, some 20,000 foreign tourists visit the country annually as of 2016.[[388]](#cite_note-399) In particular an important region for domestic and international tourism is the picturesque [Bamyan](/wiki/Bamyan "Bamyan") Valley, which includes lakes, canyons and historical sites, helped by the fact it is in a safe area away from insurgent activity.[[389]](#cite_note-400)[[390]](#cite_note-401) Smaller numbers visit and trek in regions such as the [Wakhan](/wiki/Wakhan "Wakhan") Valley, which is also one of the world's most remote communities.[[391]](#cite_note-402) From the late 1960s onwards, Afghanistan was a popular stop on the famous [hippie trail](/wiki/Hippie_trail "Hippie trail"), attracting many Europeans and Americans. Coming from Iran, the trail traveled through various Afghan provinces and cities including [Herat](/wiki/Herat "Herat"), [Kandahar](/wiki/Kandahar "Kandahar") and [Kabul](/wiki/Kabul "Kabul") before crossing to northern Pakistan, northern India, and [Nepal](/wiki/Nepal "Nepal").[[392]](#cite_note-403)[[393]](#cite_note-404) Tourism peaked in 1977, the year before the start of political instability and armed conflict.[[394]](#cite_note-405)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/86/Jam_afghanistan_ghorprovince_islamic_architecture.jpg/250px-Jam_afghanistan_ghorprovince_islamic_architecture.jpg)](/wiki/File:Jam_afghanistan_ghorprovince_islamic_architecture.jpg)

The [Minaret of Jam](/wiki/Minaret_of_Jam "Minaret of Jam"), a UNESCO [World Heritage Site](/wiki/World_Heritage_Site "World Heritage Site")

The city of [Ghazni](/wiki/Ghazni "Ghazni") has significant history and historical sites, and together with [Bamyan](/wiki/Bamyan "Bamyan") city have in recent years been voted Islamic Cultural Capital and South Asia Cultural Capital respectively.[[395]](#cite_note-406) The cities of [Herat](/wiki/Herat "Herat"), [Kandahar](/wiki/Kandahar "Kandahar"), [Balkh](/wiki/Balkh "Balkh"), and [Zaranj](/wiki/Zaranj "Zaranj") are also very historic. The [Minaret of Jam](/wiki/Minaret_of_Jam "Minaret of Jam") in the [Hari River](/wiki/Hari_River,_Afghanistan "Hari River, Afghanistan") valley is a [UNESCO World Heritage Site](/wiki/UNESCO_World_Heritage_Site "UNESCO World Heritage Site"). A cloak reputedly worn by Islam's prophet [Muhammad](/wiki/Muhammad "Muhammad") is kept in the [Shrine of the Cloak](/wiki/Shrine_of_the_Cloak "Shrine of the Cloak") in Kandahar, a city founded by [Alexander the Great](/wiki/Alexander_the_Great "Alexander the Great") and the first capital of Afghanistan. The [citadel of Alexander](/wiki/Citadel_of_Alexander "Citadel of Alexander") in the western city of Herat has been renovated in recent years and is a popular attraction. In the north of the country is the [Shrine of Ali](/wiki/Shrine_of_Ali "Shrine of Ali"), believed by many to be the location where [Ali](/wiki/Ali "Ali") was buried.[[396]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDupree1997115-407) The [National Museum of Afghanistan](/wiki/National_Museum_of_Afghanistan "National Museum of Afghanistan") in Kabul hosts a large number of Buddhist, [Bactrian](/wiki/Bactria "Bactria") Greek and early Islamic antiquities; the museum suffered greatly by civil war but has been slowly restoring since the early 2000s.[[397]](#cite_note-408)

Unexpectedly, tourism has seen improvement in Afghanistan following the Taliban takeover. Active efforts by the Taliban encouraged tourism to increase from 691 tourists in 2021, to 2,300 in 2022, to 5,200 in 2023, with some estimates of between 7,000 and 10,000.[[398]](#cite_note-409)[[399]](#cite_note-410)[[400]](#cite_note-411) This is, however, threatened by the [Islamic State – Khorasan Province](/wiki/Islamic_State_%E2%80%93_Khorasan_Province "Islamic State – Khorasan Province"), who took responsibility for attacks on tourists, such as the [2024 Bamyan shooting](/wiki/2024_Bamyan_shooting "2024 Bamyan shooting").[[401]](#cite_note-412)

### Communication

Main article: [Communications in Afghanistan](/wiki/Communications_in_Afghanistan "Communications in Afghanistan")

Telecommunication services in Afghanistan are provided by [Afghan Telecom](/wiki/Afghan_Telecom "Afghan Telecom"), [Afghan Wireless](/wiki/Afghan_Wireless "Afghan Wireless"), [Etisalat](/wiki/Etisalat "Etisalat"), [MTN Group](/wiki/MTN_Group "MTN Group"), and [Roshan](/wiki/Roshan_(telco) "Roshan (telco)"). The country uses its own space [satellite](/wiki/Satellite "Satellite") called [Afghansat 1](/wiki/Afghansat_1 "Afghansat 1"), which provides services to millions of phone, internet, and television subscribers. By 2001 following years of civil war, telecommunications was virtually a non-existent sector, but by 2016 it had grown to a $2 billion industry, with 22 million mobile phone subscribers and 5 million internet users. The sector employs at least 120,000 people nationwide.[[402]](#cite_note-413)

### Transportation

Main article: [Transport in Afghanistan](/wiki/Transport_in_Afghanistan "Transport in Afghanistan")

See also: [List of airports in Afghanistan](/wiki/List_of_airports_in_Afghanistan "List of airports in Afghanistan") and [Rail transport in Afghanistan](/wiki/Rail_transport_in_Afghanistan "Rail transport in Afghanistan")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5a/Fran%C3%A7oise_Foliot_-_Afghanistan_043.jpg/250px-Fran%C3%A7oise_Foliot_-_Afghanistan_043.jpg)](/wiki/File:Fran%C3%A7oise_Foliot_-_Afghanistan_043.jpg)

The [Salang Tunnel](/wiki/Salang_Tunnel "Salang Tunnel")

Due to Afghanistan's geography, transport between various parts of the country has historically been difficult. The backbone of Afghanistan's road network is [Highway 1](/wiki/Highway_1_(Afghanistan) "Highway 1 (Afghanistan)"), often called the "Ring Road", which extends for 2,210 kilometres (1,370 mi) and connects five major cities: Kabul, Ghazni, Kandahar, Herat and Mazar-i-Sharif,[[403]](#cite_note-414) with spurs to Kunduz and Jalalabad and various border crossings, while skirting around the mountains of the Hindu Kush.[[404]](#cite_note-415)

The Ring Road is crucially important for domestic and international trade and the economy.[[405]](#cite_note-416) A key portion of the Ring Road is the [Salang Tunnel](/wiki/Salang_Tunnel "Salang Tunnel"), completed in 1964, which facilitates travel through the Hindu Kush mountain range and connects northern and southern Afghanistan.[[406]](#cite_note-417) It is the only land route that connects Central Asia to the [Indian subcontinent](/wiki/Indian_subcontinent "Indian subcontinent").[[407]](#cite_note-418) Several mountain passes allow travel between the Hindu Kush in other areas. Serious traffic accidents are common on Afghan roads and highways, particularly on the [Kabul–Kandahar](/wiki/Kabul%E2%80%93Kandahar_Highway "Kabul–Kandahar Highway") and the [Kabul–Jalalabad Road](/wiki/Kabul%E2%80%93Jalalabad_Road "Kabul–Jalalabad Road").[[408]](#cite_note-419) Traveling by bus in Afghanistan remains dangerous due to militant activities.[[409]](#cite_note-420)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8e/Ariana_Afghan_A310-300_F-GEMO.jpg/250px-Ariana_Afghan_A310-300_F-GEMO.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ariana_Afghan_A310-300_F-GEMO.jpg)

An [Ariana Afghan Airlines](/wiki/Ariana_Afghan_Airlines "Ariana Afghan Airlines") [Airbus A310](/wiki/Airbus_A310 "Airbus A310") in 2006

Air transport in Afghanistan is provided by the national carrier, [Ariana Afghan Airlines](/wiki/Ariana_Afghan_Airlines "Ariana Afghan Airlines"),[[410]](#cite_note-421) and by the private company [Kam Air](/wiki/Kam_Air "Kam Air"). Airlines from a number of countries also provide flights in and out of the country. These include [Air India](/wiki/Air_India "Air India"), [Emirates](/wiki/Emirates_(airline) "Emirates (airline)"), [Gulf Air](/wiki/Gulf_Air "Gulf Air"), [Iran Aseman Airlines](/wiki/Iran_Aseman_Airlines "Iran Aseman Airlines"), [Pakistan International Airlines](/wiki/Pakistan_International_Airlines "Pakistan International Airlines"), and [Turkish Airlines](/wiki/Turkish_Airlines "Turkish Airlines"). The country has four international airports: [Kabul International Airport](/wiki/Kabul_International_Airport "Kabul International Airport") (formerly Hamid Karzai International Airport), [Kandahar International Airport](/wiki/Kandahar_International_Airport "Kandahar International Airport"), [Herat International Airport](/wiki/Herat_International_Airport "Herat International Airport"), and [Mazar-e Sharif International Airport](/wiki/Mazar-e_Sharif_International_Airport "Mazar-e Sharif International Airport"). Including domestic airports, there are 43.[[248]](#cite_note-Factbook-259) [Bagram Air Base](/wiki/Bagram_Air_Base "Bagram Air Base") is a major military airfield.

The country has three rail links: one, a 75-kilometre (47 mi) line from [Mazar-i-Sharif](/wiki/Mazar-i-Sharif "Mazar-i-Sharif") to the [Uzbekistan border](/wiki/Afghanistan%E2%80%93Uzbekistan_Friendship_Bridge "Afghanistan–Uzbekistan Friendship Bridge");[[411]](#cite_note-422) a 10-kilometre (6.2 mi) long line from [Toraghundi](/wiki/Toraghundi "Toraghundi") to the [Turkmenistan](/wiki/Turkmenistan "Turkmenistan") border (where it continues as part of [Turkmen Railways](/wiki/Turkmen_Railways "Turkmen Railways")); and a short link from [Aqina](/wiki/Aqina "Aqina") across the Turkmen border to [Kerki](/wiki/Kerki "Kerki"), which is planned to be extended further across Afghanistan.[[412]](#cite_note-423) These lines are used for freight only and there is no passenger service. A rail line between [Khaf](/wiki/Khaf,_Iran "Khaf, Iran"), Iran and [Herat](/wiki/Herat "Herat"), western Afghanistan, intended for both freight and passengers, was under construction as of 2019.[[413]](#cite_note-424)[[414]](#cite_note-425) About 125 kilometres (78 mi) of the line will lie on the Afghan side.[[415]](#cite_note-426)[[416]](#cite_note-427)

Private vehicle ownership has increased substantially since the early 2000s. Taxis are yellow and consist of both cars and [auto rickshaws](/wiki/Auto_rickshaw "Auto rickshaw").[[417]](#cite_note-428) In rural Afghanistan, villagers often use donkeys, [mules](/wiki/Mule "Mule") or horses to transport or carry goods. Camels are primarily used by the Kochi nomads.[[277]](#cite_note-Wahab-2007-288) Bicycles are popular throughout Afghanistan.[[418]](#cite_note-429)

## Demographics

Main article: [Demographics of Afghanistan](/wiki/Demographics_of_Afghanistan "Demographics of Afghanistan")

Further information: [Afghans](/wiki/Afghans "Afghans") and [Afghan diaspora](/wiki/Afghan_diaspora "Afghan diaspora")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/eb/CIA_Afghanistan_ethnic_groups_map.jpg/330px-CIA_Afghanistan_ethnic_groups_map.jpg)](/wiki/File:CIA_Afghanistan_ethnic_groups_map.jpg)

A Cold War-era CIA map showing traditional Afghan tribal territories. [Pashtun tribes](/wiki/Pashtun_tribes "Pashtun tribes") form the world's largest tribal society.[[419]](#cite_note-430)

### Population

The population of Afghanistan was estimated at 35.7 million as of 2024 by the Afghanistan [National Statistics and Information Authority](/wiki/National_Statistics_and_Information_Authority "National Statistics and Information Authority"),[[24]](#cite_note-NSIA_pop-31) whereas the UN estimates over 42.0 million.[[29]](#cite_note-UN_pop-36) In 1979 the total population was reported to be about 15.5 million.[[420]](#cite_note-431) About 25.3% are [urbanite](/wiki/Urban_area "Urban area"), 70.4% live in rural areas, and the remaining 4.3% are nomadic.[[24]](#cite_note-NSIA_pop-31) An additional 3 million or so Afghans are temporarily housed in neighboring [Pakistan](/wiki/Afghans_in_Pakistan "Afghans in Pakistan") and [Iran](/wiki/Afghans_in_Iran "Afghans in Iran"), most of whom were born and raised in those two countries. As of 2013, Afghanistan was the largest refugee-producing country in the world, a title held for 32 years.

The current population growth rate is 2.37%,[[248]](#cite_note-Factbook-259) one of the highest in the world outside of Africa. This population is expected to reach 82 million by 2050 if current population trends continue.[[421]](#cite_note-432) The population of Afghanistan increased steadily until the 1980s, when civil war caused millions to flee to other countries such as Pakistan.[[422]](#cite_note-433) Millions have since returned and the war conditions contribute to the country having the highest fertility rate outside Africa.[[423]](#cite_note-434) Afghanistan's healthcare has recovered since the turn of the century, causing falls in infant mortality and increases in life expectancy, although it has the lowest life expectance of any country outside Africa. This (along with other factors such as returning refugees) caused rapid population growth in the 2000s that has only recently started to slow down.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] The [Gini coefficient](/wiki/Gini_coefficient "Gini coefficient") in 2008 was 27.8.[[424]](#cite_note-435)

#### Fertility rate

Afghanistan 2024 [total fertility rate](/wiki/Total_fertility_rate "Total fertility rate") has been estimated at 4.4.[[425]](#cite_note-436) In 2022 it was 4.5, about twice the world average rate.[[426]](#cite_note-437) The rate has fallen since the early 1980s.[[427]](#cite_note-438)

### Urbanization

Main article: [List of cities in Afghanistan](/wiki/List_of_cities_in_Afghanistan "List of cities in Afghanistan")

As estimated by the CIA World Factbook, 26% of the population was urbanized as of 2020. This is one of the lowest figures in the world; in Asia it is only higher than [Cambodia](/wiki/Cambodia "Cambodia"), [Nepal](/wiki/Nepal "Nepal") and [Sri Lanka](/wiki/Sri_Lanka "Sri Lanka"). Urbanization has increased rapidly, particularly in the capital [Kabul](/wiki/Kabul "Kabul"), due to returning refugees from Pakistan and Iran after 2001, internally displaced people, and rural migrants.[[428]](#cite_note-439) Urbanization in Afghanistan is different from typical urbanization in that it is centered on just a few cities.[[429]](#cite_note-aljazeera.com-440)

Kabul is the largest city, with a population of 5 million.[[24]](#cite_note-NSIA_pop-31) The other large cities are located generally in the "ring" around the Central Highlands, namely [Kandahar](/wiki/Kandahar "Kandahar") in the south, [Herat](/wiki/Herat "Herat") in the west, [Mazar-i-Sharif](/wiki/Mazar-i-Sharif "Mazar-i-Sharif"), [Kunduz](/wiki/Kunduz "Kunduz") in the north, and [Jalalabad](/wiki/Jalalabad "Jalalabad") in the east.
